title: NOJV — Where Is The Time Going?
description: Breakdown of request time across application code (manual instrumentation) vs HTTP server (auto), Postgres client (auto), and Redis client (auto). Verified against OTel SDK 0.216 export shape: http.server.duration uses milliseconds (divided by 1000 here for s-uniform comparison); db.client.operation.duration is unified across drivers and uses db_system label to distinguish postgresql/redis. ioredis spans only appear once Redis traffic actually flows.

options.content:
## Where Is The Time Going?

Compares **manual app-level latency** (`api_request_duration_seconds` — measured around our route handlers) against **auto-instrumented component latencies** so we can attribute total request time to the right layer.

### Metric layout (verified against this stack)

| Source | Metric | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Manual handler timing | `api_request_duration_seconds_bucket` | seconds | recorded in `apps/web/src/hooks.server.ts` |
| HTTP server (auto) | `http_server_duration_milliseconds_bucket` | **milliseconds** | divide by 1000 for s-uniform comparison |
| Postgres client (auto, pg) | `db_client_operation_duration_seconds_bucket{db_system="postgresql"}` | seconds | OTel pg instrumentation |
| Redis client (auto, ioredis) | `db_client_operation_duration_seconds_bucket{db_system="redis"}` | seconds | OTel ioredis instrumentation — series only appears once Redis traffic flows |

Unified `db.client.operation.duration` covers both Postgres and Redis; the `db_system` label distinguishes drivers. Older OTel SDK versions exported `redis_client_duration_*` separately — not the case in 0.216.x.
